title: Savvy Parity Regression Model Estimation with 'savvyPR'
text: vignette demonstrates use savvyPR package parity regression model estimation cross-validation. package handles multicollinearity applying risk parity constraints, supporting Budget-based Target-based parameterizations.

title: Savvy Parity Regression Model Estimation with 'savvyPR'
code: # Install the development version from GitHub # devtools::install_github("Ziwei-ChenChen/savvyPR) library(savvyPR) library(MASS) library(glmnet) ## Loading required package: Matrix ## Loaded glmnet 4.1-8

title: Savvy Parity Regression Model Estimation with 'savvyPR'
text: savvyPR function used fit parity regression model. demonstrate usage synthetic, highly correlated data. can use summary function get detailed statistics models: package also provides built-plotting functions visualize coefficients risk parity optimization distributions.
code: library(MASS) library(glmnet)  # Function to create a correlation matrix for X create_corr_matrix <- function(rho, p) {   corr_matrix <- diag(1, p)   for (i in 2:p) {     for (j in 1:(i-1)) {       corr_matrix[i, j] <- rho^(abs(i - j))       corr_matrix[j, i] <- corr_matrix[i, j] # symmetric matrix     }   }   return(corr_matrix) }  # Function to generate beta values with both positive and negative signs generate_beta <- function(p) {   half_p <- ceiling(p / 2)   beta <- rep(c(1, -1), length.out = p) * rep(1:half_p, each = 2)[1:p]   return(beta) }  set.seed(123) n <- 1500   p <- 15   rho <- -0.5    corr_matrix <- create_corr_matrix(rho, p) x <- mvrnorm(n = n, mu = rep(0, p), Sigma = corr_matrix) beta <- generate_beta(p + 1) sigma_vec <- abs(rnorm(n = n, mean = 15, sd = sqrt(1))) y <- rnorm(n, mean = as.vector(cbind(1,x)%*%beta), sd = sigma_vec)    # 1. Run OLS estimation with intercept result_ols <- lm(y ~ x) coef_ols <- coef(result_ols)  # 2. Run Ridge Regression (RR) estimation result_RR <- glmnet(x, y, alpha = 0, lambda = 1) coef_RR <- coef(result_RR)  # 3. Run PR estimation (Budget Method) result_pr_budget <- savvyPR(x, y, method = "budget", val = 0.05, intercept = TRUE) print(result_pr_budget) ##  ## Call:  savvyPR(x = x, y = y, method = "budget", val = 0.05, intercept = TRUE)  ##  ##  Method Number of Non-Zero Coefficients Intercept Included Lambda Value ##  budget                              16                Yes           NA ##  ## Coefficients: ##  Coefficient Estimate ##  (Intercept)   0.9474 ##           X1  -3.6223 ##           X2   3.7272 ##           X3  -3.4870 ##           X4   4.1283 ##           X5  -4.1596 ##           X6   5.1385 ##           X7  -4.9644 ##           X8   6.0606 ##           X9  -6.0015 ##          X10   6.3743 ##          X11  -5.6775 ##          X12   7.8006 ##          X13  -7.3068 ##          X14   9.0372 ##          X15  -8.7735 coef_pr_budget <- coef(result_pr_budget)  # 4. Run PR estimation (Target Method) result_pr_target <- savvyPR(x, y, method = "target", val = 1, intercept = TRUE) print(result_pr_target) ##  ## Call:  savvyPR(x = x, y = y, method = "target", val = 1, intercept = TRUE)  ##  ##  Method Number of Non-Zero Coefficients Intercept Included Lambda Value ##  target                              16                Yes           NA ##  ## Coefficients: ##  Coefficient Estimate ##  (Intercept)   0.9978 ##           X1  -4.1012 ##           X2   4.1303 ##           X3  -3.8591 ##           X4   4.4168 ##           X5  -4.5009 ##           X6   5.4143 ##           X7  -5.2861 ##           X8   6.2761 ##           X9  -6.2403 ##          X10   6.5827 ##          X11  -5.9788 ##          X12   7.9785 ##          X13  -7.5766 ##          X14   9.2430 ##          X15  -9.0638 coef_pr_target <- coef(result_pr_target)  # Calculate the L2 distance to true beta ols_L2 <- sqrt(sum((beta - coef_ols)^2)) print(paste("OLS L2:", ols_L2)) ## [1] "OLS L2: 2.24654287653346" RR_L2 <- sqrt(sum((beta - coef_RR)^2)) print(paste("Ridge L2:", RR_L2)) ## [1] "Ridge L2: 2.10076303047981" pr_budget_L2 <- sqrt(sum((beta - coef_pr_budget)^2)) print(paste("PR Budget L2:", pr_budget_L2)) ## [1] "PR Budget L2: 4.6582202853575" pr_target_L2 <- sqrt(sum((beta - coef_pr_target)^2)) print(paste("PR Target L2:", pr_target_L2)) ## [1] "PR Target L2: 5.74454449168347" summary(result_pr_budget) ## Summary of Parity Model ## =================================================================== ##  ## Parameterization Method: budget  ## Intercept: Included ##  ## Call: ## savvyPR(x = x, y = y, method = "budget", val = 0.05, intercept = TRUE) ##  ## Residuals: ##          0%         25%         50%         75%        100%  ## -56.4721096 -10.9724126  -0.1952992  10.8910982  48.9682405  ##  ## Coefficients: ##             Estimate Std. Error t value  Pr(>|t|)   2.5 %   97.5 %  Signif. ## (Intercept) 0.9474   0.431      2.1984   0.0281     0.1028  1.7921  *       ## X1          -3.6223  0.5026     -7.2072  9.0577e-13 -4.6073 -2.6372 ***     ## X2          3.7272   0.5477     6.8045   1.4663e-11 2.6536  4.8007  ***     ## X3          -3.487   0.5593     -6.2346  5.8903e-10 -4.5832 -2.3908 ***     ## X4          4.1283   0.55       7.5065   1.0423e-13 3.0504  5.2062  ***     ## X5          -4.1596  0.5612     -7.4125  2.0734e-13 -5.2595 -3.0598 ***     ## X6          5.1385   0.5528     9.2957   5.0478e-20 4.0551  6.2219  ***     ## X7          -4.9644  0.5569     -8.914   1.4106e-18 -6.056  -3.8729 ***     ## X8          6.0606   0.5498     11.0227  3.2671e-27 4.983   7.1383  ***     ## X9          -6.0015  0.5598     -10.7216 6.9524e-26 -7.0986 -4.9044 ***     ## X10         6.3743   0.5607     11.3678  9.0139e-29 5.2753  7.4733  ***     ## X14         9.0372   0.5627     16.059   1.2454e-53 7.9342  10.1401 ***     ## X15         -8.7735  0.4755     -18.4495 1.3655e-68 -9.7056 -7.8415 ***     ## --- ## Signif. codes:  0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1 ##  ## Residual standard error: 16.6436 on 1484 degrees of freedom ## Multiple R-squared: 0.7826 , Adjusted R-squared: 0.7804  ## F-statistic: 356.1532 on 15 and 1484 DF,  p-value: 0.0000e+00  ## AIC: 8451.993 , BIC: 8537.004 , Deviance: 411082.4 summary(result_pr_target) ## Summary of Parity Model ## =================================================================== ##  ## Parameterization Method: target  ## Intercept: Included ##  ## Call: ## savvyPR(x = x, y = y, method = "target", val = 1, intercept = TRUE) ## title: Savvy Parity Regression Model Estimation with 'savvyPR'
text: cv.savvyPR function performs cross-validation select optimal parameters. handles “budget” sequence “target” sequence automatically. can summarize cross-validation results see optimal tuning values chosen algorithm. can also visualize cross-validated models:    can visualize cross-validation error curves see exactly optimal minimum found.     Finally, can plot Coefficient Paths see coefficients shrink change tuning parameter varies. Notice use xvar = “val” plot unified tuning parameter.

text: savvyPR package implements Parity Regression (PR), novel regularization framework designed distribute prediction error equilibrium across model parameters. methodology particularly effective high-dimensional data characterized substantial noise high multicollinearity, financial time series structural changes evolving trends. package based theoretical foundations algorithms discussed : Asimit, V., Chen, Z., Ichim, B., & Millossovich, P. (2026). Parity Regression Estimation. official documentation site available : https://ziwei-chenchen.github.io/savvyPR/

title: Savvy Parity Regression Model Estimation with savvyPR
text: Unlike standard shrinkage methods like Ridge Lasso, Parity Regression ensures single predictor dominates model’s risk profile. optimizes objective functions using two distinct parameterizations:

title: Savvy Parity Regression Model Estimation with savvyPR
text: cc-parameterization explicitly allocates fixed loss contribution predictor. objective function defined : 12RRSS(x,xp+1;λ)−μ̃(c∑k=0plog(δkxk)+(1−(p+1)c)log(xp+1))\frac{1}{2} RRSS(x, x_{p+1}; \lambda) - \tilde{\mu} \left( c \sum_{k=0}^{p} \log(\delta_k x_k) + (1 - (p + 1)c) \log(x_{p+1}) \right) ensure positive risk allocation, cc constrained 0≤c<1/(p+1)0 \le c < 1/(p+1).

title: Savvy Parity Regression Model Estimation with savvyPR
text: tt-parameterization treats tt relative elasticity weight target variable (response): 12RRSS(x,xp+1;λ)−μ̃(∑k=0plog(δkxk)+tlog(xp+1))\frac{1}{2} RRSS(x, x_{p+1}; \lambda) - \tilde{\mu} \left( \sum_{k=0}^{p} \log(\delta_k x_k) + t \log(x_{p+1}) \right)

title: Savvy Parity Regression Model Estimation with savvyPR
text: Three Model Types: PR1: Pure parity optimization (fixing λ=0\lambda=0). PR2: Parity optimization using fixed λ\lambda derived Ridge regression via cv.glmnet. PR3: Dual-stage optimization tunes parity parameter λ\lambda. Built-Cross-Validation: Automated selection optimal cc, tt, λ\lambda using cv.savvyPR. Rich Visualizations: Support plotting estimated coefficients, risk contributions, coefficient paths, CV error curves.

title: Savvy Parity Regression Model Estimation with savvyPR
code: # Generate highly correlated data set.seed(123) n <- 100; p <- 10 base <- rnorm(n) x <- matrix(rnorm(n * p, sd = 0.1), n, p) + base y <- as.numeric(x %*% rep(1, p) + rnorm(n, sd = 0.5))  # Fit a cross-validated PR3 model (Budget method) cv_fit <- cv.savvyPR(x, y, method = "budget", model_type = "PR3") coef(cv_fit)  # Plot the results plot(cv_fit, plot_type = "estimated_coefficients") plot(cv_fit, plot_type = "risk_contributions")

title: Cross-Validation for Parity Regression Model Estimation — cv.savvyPR
text: Performs k-fold cross-validation Parity Regression (PR) models select optimal tuning parameters. underlying PR methodology distributes total prediction error evenly across parameters, ensuring stability presence high multicollinearity substantial noise (time series data structural changes evolving trends). function supports Budget-based Target-based parameterizations evaluates models across variety loss metrics.

title: Cross-Validation for Parity Regression Model Estimation — cv.savvyPR
text: x matrix predictors rows observations columns variables. Must contain NA values, include intercept column ones. y numeric vector response variable, number observations x. Must contain NA values. method Character string specifying parameterization method use: "budget" (default) "target". vals Optional; numeric vector values tuning PR model (acts c budget, t target). NULL, default sequence generated based selected method. Must contain least two values. nval Numeric value specifying number tuning values try optimization process vals=NULL. Defaults 100. lambda_vals Optional; numeric vector lambda values used regularization "PR2" "PR3" model types. NULL model_type "PR2" "PR3", default sequence used. Must contain least two values. nlambda Numeric value specifying number lambda_val values try optimization process lambda_vals=NULL. folds number folds used cross-validation, default 10. Must integer >= 3. model_type Character string specifying type model fit. Defaults "PR3". Can one "PR3", "PR1", "PR2". See details clarification. measure_type Character vector specifying measure use model evaluation. Defaults "mse". Supported types include "mse", "mae", "rmse", "mape". foldid Logical indicating whether return fold assignments. Defaults FALSE. use_feature_selection Logical indicating whether perform feature selection model fitting process. Defaults FALSE. standardize Logical indicating whether standardize predictor variables. Defaults TRUE. intercept Logical indicating whether include intercept model. Defaults TRUE. exclude Optional; indicate variables excluded model fitting process.

PR_fit final fitted model object savvyPR function. coefficients_cv matrix average coefficients across cross-validation folds tuning parameter. vals tuning values (acting c t) used cross-validation process. lambda_vals lambda values used cross-validation process, applicable PR2 PR3. optimal_val optimal tuning value found cross-validation, applicable PR1 PR2. fixed_val fixed tuning value used PR3, derived initial PR1-style optimization. optimal_lambda_val optimal lambda value found PR3. fixed_lambda_val fixed lambda value used PR2, derived cv.glmnet. optimal_index list detailing indices optimal parameters within cross-validation matrix. fold_assignments (Optional) fold assignments used cross-validation, provided foldid=TRUE.

title: Cross-Validation for Parity Regression Model Estimation — cv.savvyPR
text: Cross-Validation Parity Regression Model Estimation function facilitates cross-validation parity regression models across range tuning values (val) regularization values (\(\lambda\)), depending model type specified. model type handles parameters differently: PR1 Performs cross-validation val sequence   fixing \(\lambda=0\). model type primarily used focus   understanding different levels risk parity constraints impact   model performance purely based parity mechanism without influence   ridge \(\lambda\) shrinkage. PR2 Uses fixed \(\lambda\) value determined performing ridge   regression (lambda optimization) using cv.glmnet   dataset. performs cross-validation val sequence   using optimized \(\lambda\) value. approach useful   one wishes maintain stable amount standard shrinkage exploring   impact varying levels proportional contribution constraint. PR3 First, determines optimal val using method PR1.   , keeping val fixed, conducts cross-validation   possible \(\lambda\) values. dual-stage optimization can particularly   effective initial parity regularization needs refinement   via \(\lambda\) adjustment. function supports several types loss metrics assessing model performance: mse Mean Squared Error: Measures average squares errors—,   average squared difference estimated values actual value. mae Mean Absolute Error: Measures average magnitude errors   set predictions, without considering direction. ’s average   test sample absolute differences prediction actual observation   individual differences equal weight. rmse Root Mean Squared Error: square root mean squared   errors. title: Parity Regression Model Estimation — savvyPR
text: Implements Parity Regression (PR) methodology multiple linear regression. Instead minimizing aggregate prediction error dependent variable, PR distributes total prediction error evenly across parameters. approach ensures stability presence high multicollinearity particularly suitable data affected substantial noise, time series data experiencing structural changes evolving trends.

title: Parity Regression Model Estimation — savvyPR
text: x matrix predictors rows observations columns variables. Must contain NA values, include intercept column ones. y numeric vector response variable, number observations x. Must contain NA values. method Character string specifying parameterization method use: "budget" (default) "target". val Numeric tuning parameter. method = "budget", represents c (value 0 1/p). method = "target", represents t (target risk parameter > 0). lambda_val Optional; numeric value specifying regularization strength. NULL, lambda determined via cross-validation. use_feature_selection Logical; TRUE, applies Lasso perform feature selection model estimation. Defaults FALSE. standardize Logical; TRUE, scale center predictor variables fitting model. Defaults TRUE. intercept Logical; TRUE, includes intercept model, otherwise, model estimate intercept term. Defaults TRUE. exclude Optional; array indices specifying columns excluded analysis.

title: Parity Regression Model Estimation — savvyPR
text: Returns S3 object class "savvyPR" containing following components: call matched call function. coefficients numeric vector estimated coefficients. tuning parameter zero, coefficients obtained Ridge regression OLS. Otherwise, obtained parity regression model. method optimization method used ("budget" "target"). fit fitted object returned glmnet tuning parameter zero. orp_fit fitted object returned risk parity optimizer (optimizeRiskParityBudget optimizeRiskParityTarget) tuning parameter non-zero. lambda regularization parameter lambda used model. intercept logical value indicating whether intercept included model. model data frame containing response variable y covariates x used model.

title: Parity Regression Model Estimation — savvyPR
text: Parity Regression Model Estimation PR methodology assigns equal risk contribution constraint predictor variable within specific search cone, leading robust solution. solution defined context penalized regression, wherein penalty term function regularization parameter (lambda_val) proportional contribution parameter (val). function uses nleqslv package solve non-linear systems equations. function supports two parameterization methods: Budget: Uses val (acting c) set strict budget constraint risk contributions. Target: Uses val (acting t) set risk target response variable relative predictors. function can handle different practical scenarios: PR theorem specifically designed variable selection, package makes available optional preprocessing step.  use_feature_selection TRUE, Lasso regression performed select features zeroing non-contributive predictors applying PR model. checks matrix rank predictors applies Ridge regression fallback ordinary least squares matrix full rank, ensuring computational stability. budget method, PR methodology optimizes objective function includes variance term penalization term: $$1/2 * RRSS(x, x_{p+1}; \lambda) - \widetilde{\mu} ( c \sum_{k=0}^p \log(\delta_k x_k) + (1 - (p+1)c) \log(x_{p+1}) )$$ target method, methodology optimizes related objective function defined target parameter \(t\): $$1/2 * RRSS(x, x_{p+1}; \lambda) - \widetilde{\mu} ( \sum_{k=0}^p \log(\delta_k x_k) + t \log(x_{p+1}) )$$ formulas, \(x\) represents parameters (\(x_{p+1}\) auxiliary parameter set 1), \(\lambda\) regularization parameter, \(p\) number predictors, \(\widetilde{\mu}\) constant respect \(\lambda\). resulting model provides estimates regression coefficients equitable across predictors terms contribution model's predictive power.

title: Parity Regression Model Estimation — savvyPR
code: library(glmnet) #> Loading required package: Matrix #> Loaded glmnet 4.1-8 library(nleqslv)  # Generate synthetic data set.seed(123) n <- 100 # Number of observations p <- 10  # Number of variables x <- matrix(rnorm(n * p), n, p) beta <- matrix(rnorm(p), p, 1) y <- x %*% beta + rnorm(n, sd = 0.5) # Linear combination with noise  # Example 1: Run PR estimation using the "budget" method (acting as c) result_budget <- savvyPR(x, y, method = "budget", val = 0.05, intercept = TRUE) print(result_budget$coefficients) #>  [1]  0.04997937 -1.00500454 -1.10097610  0.12403465 -0.14222097 -2.62920376 #>  [7]  1.07610919  0.29923597  2.36165735  0.68467176 -0.44474216  # Example 2: Run PR estimation using the "target" method (acting as t) result_target <- savvyPR(x, y, method = "target", val = 1, intercept = TRUE) print(result_target$coefficients) #>  [1]  0.04468683 -1.01944158 -1.11908908  0.17409082 -0.18240697 -2.63781293 #>  [7]  1.08697719  0.33274074  2.37947640  0.69904692 -0.46125508  # Example 3: Run PR estimation with feature selection result_fs <- savvyPR(x, y, method = "budget", val = 0.05, use_feature_selection = TRUE) print(result_fs$coefficients) #>  [1]  0.04997937 -1.00500454 -1.10097610  0.12403465 -0.14222097 -2.62920376 #>  [7]  1.07610919  0.29923597  2.36165735  0.68467176 -0.44474216  # Inspect the risk parity portfolio object for more details if(!is.null(result_fs$orp_fit)) {   print("Risk parity portfolio details:")   print(result_fs$orp_fit) } #> [1] "Risk parity portfolio details:" #> $weights #>  [1] 0.09247496 0.10130573 0.01141298 0.01308639 0.24192478 0.09901761 #>  [7] 0.02753404 0.21730664 0.06299971 0.04092271 0.09201447 #>  #> $relativeRiskContrib #>  [1] 0.05 0.05 0.05 0.05 0.05 0.05 0.05 0.05 0.05 0.05 0.50 #>  #> $objFunction #> [1] 2.608521 #>  #> $isFeasible #> [1] TRUE #>  #> $message #> [1] "Optimization successful" #>   # Example 4: Run PR estimation excluding some predictors result_exclude <- savvyPR(x, y, method = "budget", val = 0.05, exclude = c(1, 2)) print("Coefficients with first two predictors excluded:") #> [1] "Coefficients with first two predictors excluded:" print(result_exclude$coefficients) #> [1]  0.004400731  0.409631245 -0.322373524 -2.327305565  1.014530237 #> [6]  0.448410716  2.381883669  0.865958692 -0.489070522

title: Summary of Cross-Validated Parity Regression Model — summary.cv.savvyPR
text: Summary Fitted Cross-Validated Parity Regression Model Statistics function S3 method generic summary function. formats prints detailed statistical overview optimal cross-validated model. output includes: parameterization method used ("budget" "target"). matched call produced model. Residual quantiles. table estimated optimal coefficients corresponding standard errors, t-values, p-values, confidence intervals, significance codes. Overall model fit statistics, including Residual Standard Error, Multiple Adjusted R-squared, F-statistic, AIC, BIC, Deviance. Cross-Validation Summary displaying minimum mean cross-validation error optimal tuning values (val /lambda).
